Gas humans need to breathe?

Gas plants absorb during photosynthesis?

Powerhouse of the cell?
Largest planet in our solar system?
Force that keeps us on the ground?

Boiling point of water in Fahrenheit?

Hardest natural substance?
How many bones in the human hand?
Largest organ in the human body?
What causes thunder?

200.q: Chemical formula for table salt?
Gas making up most of the air we breathe?
Unit of measuring electrical current?
Center of an atom?
What is a herbivore?
200.a: An animal that only eats plants

200.q: Chemical symbol for carbon?
Water turning into steam?
What is photosynthesis?
200.a: Process by which plants convert sunlight, CO₂ and water into glucose and oxygen

200.q: How many planets in our solar system?
What is a black hole?
200.a: A region of spacetime with gravity so strong nothing — not even light — can escape

200.q: What is the function of red blood cells?
200.a: Carrying oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body

200.q: What are the three states of matter?
200.a: Solid, liquid, and gas (plasma is the fourth)

200.q: What is a neutron?
200.a: A subatomic particle in the nucleus with no electric charge

400.q: What is the basic unit of heredity?
What blood cells help your blood clot?
What acid is found in the stomach?
What scale is commonly used to rate tornado strength?
What part of the atom has a positive charge?
What is the pH of pure water?
What particle has a negative charge?
What is Newton's first law also known as?
How long does light from the Sun take to reach Earth?
What is the chemical symbol for sodium?
What is the hardest mineral on the Mohs scale?
What type of rock is formed by cooling lava?
What is the process of water turning to vapor?
What metal is the best conductor of electricity?
What is the most abundant element in the universe?
What is the name of the galaxy we live in?
What organ filters blood in the human body?
What scientist proposed the three laws of motion?
What type of wave is light?

400.a: For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction
Energy equals mass times the speed of light squared — mass and energy are equivalent
Change in frequency of a wave for an observer moving relative to its source
Filtering toxins, producing bile, metabolizing nutrients — over 500 functions
Bacteria are living organisms; viruses are not — they need a host cell to replicate
Gene-editing technology that can precisely cut and modify DNA sequences
Fight infection and disease as part of the immune system
Splitting of a heavy atomic nucleus into smaller ones, releasing enormous energy
The habitable zone around a star where liquid water can exist on a planet's surface
Process by which carbon moves through Earth's systems — atmosphere, oceans, land, and organisms
The range of all electromagnetic radiation — from radio waves to gamma rays including visible light
An enormous stellar explosion when a massive star exhausts its fuel and collapses

400.q: What is the continental drift theory?
400.a: Continents were once joined as Pangaea and slowly moved apart over millions of years
An object at rest stays at rest; an object in motion stays in motion unless acted on by an external force
Production and emission of light by a living organism through chemical reactions
Study of heritable changes in gene expression that don't involve changes to the DNA sequence
Every particle has wave properties and every wave has particle properties
An incredibly dense stellar remnant — a teaspoon would weigh billions of tonnes
European Organization for Nuclear Research — home of the Large Hadron Collider in Geneva

400.q: What is the immune system's role?
400.a: Protect the body against pathogens through a network of cells and proteins

600.q: What law links gas pressure and volume inversely?
600.a: Boyle's law

600.q: What is the SI unit of frequency?
600.a: Hertz

600.q: What branch of science studies the atmosphere and weather?
600.a: Meteorology

600.q: What is the study of fossils called?
600.a: Paleontology

600.q: What force opposes motion between surfaces?
600.a: Friction

600.q: What blood cells help clotting?
600.a: Platelets

600.q: What part of the brain controls balance?
600.a: Cerebellum

600.q: What is the SI unit of power?
600.a: Watt

600.q: What gas do red blood cells carry away from the body?
600.a: Carbon dioxide

600.q: What organelle contains a cell's genetic material?
600.a: Nucleus

600.q: What branch of biology studies insects?
600.a: Entomology

600.q: What process turns liquid into gas?
600.a: Evaporation

600.q: What is the common name for sodium bicarbonate?
600.a: Baking soda

600.q: What is Schrödinger's cat used to illustrate?
600.a: Quantum superposition

600.q: What is the speed of sound in air at sea level?
600.a: 343 m/s (about 767 mph)

600.q: What force holds the nucleus of an atom together?
600.a: Strong nuclear force

600.q: What is the Chandrasekhar limit?
600.a: 1.4 solar masses (max mass for a white dwarf)

600.q: What is the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle?
600.a: You cannot simultaneously know exact position and momentum of a particle

600.q: What is the Doppler effect?
600.a: Change in frequency of waves relative to a moving observer

600.q: What is the Krebs cycle?
600.a: A series of chemical reactions in cellular respiration

600.q: What is Avogadro's number?
What does CRISPR stand for?
600.a: Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats

600.q: What is the wave-particle duality?
600.a: Light and matter exhibit both wave and particle properties

600.q: What is dark energy?
600.a: A mysterious force causing the universe's accelerating expansion

600.q: What is the Oort Cloud?
600.a: A theoretical shell of icy objects surrounding the solar system

600.q: What is the difference between fission and fusion?
600.a: Fission splits atoms; fusion combines them

600.q: What is entropy in thermodynamics?
600.a: A measure of disorder in a system

600.q: What is quantum entanglement?
600.a: A phenomenon where linked particles affect each other across distance

600.q: What does the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle state?
600.a: You cannot simultaneously know the exact position and momentum of a particle

600.q: What is the Chandrasekhar limit?
600.a: ~1.4 solar masses — the maximum mass of a stable white dwarf

600.q: What is quantum tunnelling?
600.a: Particles pass through energy barriers that classical physics says they cannot cross

600.q: What is the Standard Model in physics?
600.a: Theory describing fundamental particles and forces except gravity

600.q: What is dark matter?
600.a: Undetected matter (~27% of universe) that doesn't interact with light

600.q: What is Schrödinger's Cat?
600.a: A thought experiment showing quantum superposition — a cat is simultaneously alive and dead until observed

600.q: What is the Pauli Exclusion Principle?
600.a: No two identical fermions can occupy the same quantum state simultaneously

600.q: What is the theory of punctuated equilibrium?
600.a: Evolution occurs in rapid bursts separated by long periods of stability

600.q: What is the cosmological constant?
600.a: Einstein's term added to his equations — now linked to dark energy driving expansion

600.q: What is the Hubble constant?
600.a: The rate at which the universe is expanding — approximately 70 km/s per megaparsec

600.q: What is nuclear fusion?
600.a: Combining light atomic nuclei to release energy — the process powering stars

600.q: What is Maxwell's demon?
600.a: Thought experiment proposing a demon could violate thermodynamics by sorting molecules — information theory resolves it

600.q: What is DNA's double helix?
600.a: The spiral structure of DNA — discovered by Watson and Crick using Rosalind Franklin's X-ray data in 1953

600.q: What is the significance of the photoelectric effect?
600.a: Einstein's explanation that light comes in discrete packets (photons) — laid the foundation for quantum mechanics

600.q: What is the Higgs boson?
600.a: The particle that gives other particles mass — confirmed in 2012 at CERN

600.q: What is the measurement problem in QM?
600.a: Why quantum measurements produce definite outcomes when QM describes states as superpositions

600.q: What is Bell's theorem?
600.a: Proves no local hidden variable theory can reproduce all quantum mechanical predictions

600.q: What is a quasar?
600.a: An extremely luminous active galactic nucleus powered by a supermassive black hole

600.q: What is the Stern-Gerlach experiment?
600.a: Proved particles have quantized angular momentum (spin) — key proof of quantum mechanics

600.q: What is the Banach-Tarski paradox?
600.a: A mathematical theorem that a sphere can be decomposed and reassembled into two identical spheres

600.q: What is entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics?
600.a: Entropy (disorder) always increases in a closed system — the universe tends toward disorder

600.q: What is the significance of Proxima Centauri b?
600.a: A potentially habitable exoplanet orbiting Proxima Centauri — our nearest stellar neighbor
